commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=a99deae73bd73028a8c0fbbef091681d21956dd7)
commands.review_summary:
The PR changes embedded attempt session-lock fencing to count user-role JSONL messages on stat mismatch and adds a unit test for assistant/tool appends during the released prompt window.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level reproduction is high-confidence: current main snapshots a stat fingerprint before prompt I/O, while direct transcript appends can change size/mtime without adding a user turn. I did not run a live channel reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: This needs contributor real behavior proof plus a source-branch revision to the fence semantics; ClawSweeper should not open a repair lane while the proof gate remains contributor-owned.

target.title: fix(agents): count user turns instead of stat for embedded session takeover fence
target.body:
## Summary

The embedded attempt session-lock fence uses a `stat()`-based fingerprint (`dev`, `ino`, `size`, `mtimeNs`, `ctimeNs`) to detect "another lane queued a new user turn against this session" while the runner releases its coarse lock for the LLM prompt window. The fingerprint is a proxy that overfires: every transcript-append the runner itself performs during the released window — `appendSessionTranscriptMessageLocked` writes tool calls, tool results, and assistant replies via `acquireSessionWriteLock(..., allowReentrant: true)` — changes the file's `size`/`mtimeNs`/`ctimeNs` without going through `refreshSessionFileFence`. Long DM turns with tool calls then trip `assertSessionFileFence` and throw `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` after the reply has already been delivered, surfacing a misleading "Agent failed before reply" message on top of a successful turn.

The true takeover signal is a new `role: "user"` message entry appearing during the released window. This PR keeps the fast-path stat fingerprint but, on mismatch, disambiguates by counting `{type: "message", message.role: "user"}` entries — the runner's own assistant/tool transcript writes leave that count unchanged and refresh the fingerprint silently; a genuine new user turn still trips the takeover.

Refs:
- Closes #83436
- Closes #83615

## Verification

`Behavior addressed`: Embedded runner false-positive `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` after long DM turns whose reply was delivered successfully.

`Real environment tested`: Local Linux checkout on Node 24.13.0; targeted Vitest on the session-lock module.

`Exact steps or command run after this patch`:
```
CI=true pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts
```

`Evidence after fix`:
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
   Start at  03:54:01
   Duration  1.72s
```

`Observed result after fix`: The renamed existing test (`rejects post-prompt writes when another owner queues a new user turn`) still asserts the takeover when a `role: "user"` entry is appended during the released window. The new test (`accepts post-prompt writes when only the runner's own transcript entries appear`) covers appending assistant + tool entries during the released window and asserts the fence does NOT trip and `hasSessionTakeover()` stays `false`.

`What was not tested`: Atomic file-replacement (dev/ino swap) during the released window with an unchanged user-message count — left to upstream review whether that needs a separate disambiguation; the stat fingerprint still detects the inode change via the fast-path mismatch.

## Notes

- Adds one `countUserMessageEntries(sessionFile)` helper colocated with `readSessionFileFingerprint`. Tolerates `ENOENT` and malformed JSONL lines (partial writes), matching the file's existing read-style.
- Adds one piece of state (`fenceUserMessageCount`) to the controller closure; populated on `releaseForPrompt` and `refreshSessionFileFence`.
- Existing test renamed for clarity and updated to use a payload that matches the new fence semantics. No new fixtures.
target.author: kays0x
